title: Deploy: run full pre-deploy verification and tag next version
description: Invoked via /deploy skill. Must run scripts/pre-deploy to completion. If passes, determine next patch version from tags, show changes since last tag, get confirmation, then tag and push. Use bd for tracking only. Follow repo rules: no branches, trunk-based, full gates. Parent context from prior work on tenun-mcp but this is main app deploy.
notes: Pre-deploy ran but FAILED. 11 test failures in SlackexWeb.Channels.EnvelopeContractTest (DMChannel and ChatChannel tests). Root cause: Ecto.Adapters.SQL.Sandbox.start_owner!/2 badmatch :already_shared in data_case.ex:45 and channel_case setups. This matches known project test isolation issues (RCA 27250196864, test_teardown_safety_test.exs enforcement of no on_exit DB writes, no Sandbox.mode in tests, durable owner for ChannelServer). Script exited non-zero per set -e after mix test failures (29 tests run? partial in tail, 11 failures, 1650 excluded). Docker infra was up per prior. NO TAGGING. Latest tag still v0.9.26. Recent commits include tenun MCP plan beads work + prior. Unstaged .beads changes present (from previous session). Recommend fixing test isolation or running full gates in clean env before any deploy. Stopping per deploy skill.

title: Edit Profile: nested form detaches Save button
description: A nested <form> for the notification-level selector (added by c43bcf5) sits inside #edit-profile-form. Invalid HTML: the browser closes the profile form early at the inner </form>, detaching the Save submit button. Result: save_profile never fires, so status doesn't persist and the modal never dismisses. Fix: replace nested <form> with a bare <select phx-change=update_notification_level>. Regression test added (only render_submit-by-id bypassed it, plus :push_notifications off in test).
close_reason: Fixed in ddb521b — unnested notification-level form; regression test added

title: App /metrics endpoint p99 1.2s, tail >10s — causes Prometheus 'down' blips
description: Prometheus up{job=slackex} shows ~7 single-scrape failures/day/node (escalated from ~2-9/day to 15-32/day since 2026-06-09). Root cause isolated: scrape_duration_seconds for app1/app2 has p99=1.2s and max=10.0s (the scrape timeout) over 7d, vs 1-2ms for otel-collector. The payload is tiny (175 lines), so it's not render size — MetricsExporter calls TelemetryMetricsPrometheus.Core.scrape(:slackex_metrics) (GenServer call doing on-scrape aggregation); suspect distribution-metric sample aggregation cost spiking with event volume, or registry GenServer contention. /health stays <3s throughout (Caddy active probes by IP: 0 failures in 7d outside deploy), so user traffic is fine — this only breaks the scrape SLO and makes up{} dashboards lie. Investigate: time Core.scrape/1 in prod iex under load; check distribution sample volume between scrapes; consider raising scrape_timeout as a stopgap and check what changed around Jun 9 (factory MCP traffic?).

title: AnalyticsPlug crashes on every request: get_session without fetch_session
description: AnalyticsPlug (endpoint.ex:62) calls get_session/2 but only Plug.Session (config, line 61) runs before it — fetch_session/2 lives in the router :browser pipeline, which runs after endpoint plugs. So ensure_session_id raises ArgumentError 'session not fetched' on EVERY request; the rescue (added in 3a3ca20 'rescue all errors') swallows it and the before_send page-view callback is never registered. HTTP-level page-view tracking is dead; page_view events still appear in analytics_events (5689 rows, fresh as of 2026-06-12) via a parallel path (likely LiveView), so the feature is partially degraded, not fully dead. Prod logs show the warning every ~5s (one per scrape/request). Fix: call fetch_session(conn, []) in the plug (or move it after a fetch_session plug), then remove the broad rescue per the no-silent-failure rule. Pre-existing since ~v0.8.10, NOT introduced by v0.9.27.

title: Docs + rollout: update architecture, promote spec, operator runbook, final verification
description:
Parent: slackex-cdi

title: MCP names: update tool descriptions, server instructions, and add small helpers
description:
Parent: slackex-cdi

Make the numeric IDs less painful for agents and coordinators:

- Update relevant prompts (summarize_channel, draft_spec) if they reference channels.

title: MCP names: include channel_name and channel_slug in message/search payloads
description:
Parent: slackex-cdi

From the 2026-06-12 plan, Slice 2.

Improve agent context by denormalizing channel identity into common responses:
- Extend Serializer.message/1 and message_from_map/1 to include channel_name and channel_slug (when available from the loaded data or search result).
- Ensure search_messages responses (via Slackex.Search) carry the enriched shape.
- Keep backward compatible (additive fields only).
- Update any places that build message maps (e.g. from ChannelServer batch writes).
- Add serializer contract tests for the new fields.

Acceptance: When an agent does search_messages (broad or scoped) or receives send_message/reply results, the returned objects contain channel_name and channel_slug alongside channel_id. No extra round-trips needed for human context.

Priority: P2

title: MCP names: bot-scoped list_channels tool/resource with human names
description:
Parent: slackex-cdi

From the 2026-06-12 plan, Slice 2 (main names win).

Implement a convenient way for an authenticated MCP bot to discover the channels it can act in, with human-readable names:
- New tool 'list_channels' (or 'list_my_channels') or enhanced resource that returns only channels where the session.bot_user has a Subscription (i.e. member channels).
- Must return the rich Serializer.channel shape (id + name + slug + description + member_count + inserted_at).
- This is more useful to agents than the global public 'tenun:///channels' list.
- Update the MCP server (tools or resources) and any necessary Chat context query (reuse/extend list_user_channels or add bot-specific variant).
- Add corresponding serializer contract test.

Acceptance: Agent calls the mechanism and receives entries containing both numeric id and human name/slug for channels it is actually subscribed to. Works for freshly subscribed bots.

Priority: P2

title: Plan: Tenun MCP bot subscription completion + human-readable channel names
description:
User request to create implementation plan for two related Tenun/MCP gaps: 1) Complete usable in-app bot channel subscription (slash commands per 2026-06-06 spec) so MCP bots can be granted access to channels like 1/4/5 without seeding. 2) Better exposure of human-readable channel names (name+slug already in Serializer.channel and /channels resource, but not convenient for agents in search results, tool responses, or bot-scoped lists; forces ID memorization). 

Scope includes: audit current implementation vs spec, fill gaps in LiveView handler/Members/MCP surface, enhance discoverability (bot-scoped channel list with names, include names in message/search payloads or add helper, improve tool docs), full integration test proving subscribe unlocks MCP writes, flag discipline, docs updates (MCP arch, tenun polish), bd tracking for slices. 

Respect: dark shipping behind :bot_subscription, CLAUDE.md (Gather/Decide/Act if new logic, ghost handling, no on_exit writes, integration tests for wiring), pre-deploy gates, architecture docs.

Commit dcb5d44. Ready to mark done per acceptance (end-to-end subscribe->agent uses name+id verified+documented).

Worktree feature branch pushed + clean per finishing. All per plan + skill + repo rules (bd, CLAUDE, worktree, TDD, real MCP, etc.).

title: Owner-driven MCP bot channel subscription via /subscribe-bot slash command
description: MCP bot users are not auto-subscribed to any channel, so an agent harness authenticated as the bot connects but every write tool fails check_membership with 'Not a member of this channel'. Add in-chat, owner-driven /subscribe-bot <name> and /unsubscribe-bot <name> slash commands (flag-gated behind :bot_subscription) to add/remove an existing MCP bot's subscription row in the active public channel. No new tables, no token handling. Full design: docs/superpowers/specs/2026-06-06-bot-channel-subscription-design.md
design:
# Bot Channel Subscription Design Spec

**Date:** 2026-06-06
**Status:** Draft
**Feature Flag:** `:bot_subscription`

## Overview

MCP bot users are not auto-subscribed to any channel (unlike webhook bots, which are subscribed atomically at webhook-creation time). An agent harness authenticated as an MCP bot can therefore connect successfully but every write tool — `send_message`, `reply_to_thread`, `react_to_message` — fails the membership check with `"Not a member of this channel"` until the bot is subscribed by some out-of-band means.

This spec adds an in-chat, owner-driven way to subscribe (and unsubscribe) an existing MCP bot to a channel via two slash commands: `/subscribe-bot <name>` and `/unsubscribe-bot <name>`. The command runs server-side in the channel LiveView as the logged-in owner, resolves the bot by name, and inserts (or deletes) a `subscriptions` row for that bot user in the active channel. No new settings surface, no new tables, no token handling.

The mental model the command implements:

```
ONE bot user (minted in IEx)  +  ONE harness connection (hermes mcp add)  +  N channel subscriptions (/subscribe-bot ×N)
```

Minting the token and configuring the harness happen once and are out of scope here. `/subscribe-bot` is the only repeated step — once per channel — and each invocation adds a single membership row for the one pre-existing, pre-authenticated bot user.

## Constraints

- ~50 active users (homelab/team scale); typically a single operator running one Claude Code MCP bot
- The command must not mint, store, or return any token — the MCP bearer is show-once and only its SHA-256 hash is persisted (see `docs/architecture/integrations.md` §6.1)
- Subscription is pure authorization: it references the bot user created at token-mint time; it never creates a user
- Public channels only — mirrors the webhook subscription constraint (`private_channel_not_supported`)
- Owner-driven and feature-flagged behind `:bot_subscription`
- Reuses the existing role model (`subscriptions.role`) and authorization seam (`manage_members`) — no new permission concepts

## Background: What Already Exists

| Component | Status | Notes |
|-----------|--------|-------|
| `ChatLive.SlashCommand` | Exists | Pure parser: string → tagged tuple. Extensible via `do_parse/1` clauses. Currently handles `/summarize`, `/decide`. |
| `ChatLive.Index` `"send_message"` handler | Exists | Pattern-matches the parsed tuple (`index.ex:350`). Has `current_user`, active channel, and flag context in scope. `/decide` (`index.ex:359`) is the template for a flag-gated, owner-context command. |
| `Chat.Members` | Exists | `update_member_role/4`, `kick_member/3`, `list_members/1` — all gated on `manage_members`. **No `add_member`-style function exists yet.** |
| `Chat.Permissions.can?/2` | Exists | Role hierarchy: owner(4) > admin(3) > member(2) > viewer(1). `manage_members` requires level 3 (admin+). |
| `Integrations.Webhooks.subscribe_bot/2` | Exists | Proven `Subscription` insert with `role: "member"`, `on_conflict: :nothing`, with the nil-id ghost-struct guard. The pattern to lift. |
| `Accounts.search_users/2` | Exists | Trigram user search. Used to resolve the bot by name. |
| Bot user (`is_bot: true`, username `mcp-<name>`) | Exists | Created atomically at `McpTokens.create_mcp_token/1` time. The subscription target. |

## Why a Slash Command (Not a Settings Surface)

Channels currently have no settings concept at all (the schema is `name`, `slug`, `description`, `is_private`). A slash command keeps the entire flow inside the chat UI where the owner already is, reuses the existing two-stage parse → handle seam, and avoids building a channel-settings page solely to host one toggle. It also runs server-side as `socket.assigns.current_user`, so the owner identity is trustworthy and authorization is enforced where the rest of `Chat.Members` enforces it.

## Command Surface

### Parse Stage (`ChatLive.SlashCommand`)

Add `do_parse/1` clauses mirroring the existing `{:summarize, range}` shape (command takes one argument):

```elixir
["subscribe-bot", bot_name]   -> {:subscribe_bot, String.trim(bot_name)}
["subscribe-bot"]             -> {:subscribe_bot_help}
["unsubscribe-bot", bot_name] -> {:unsubscribe_bot, String.trim(bot_name)}
["unsubscribe-bot"]           -> {:unsubscribe_bot_help}
```

The `_help` variants (no argument) flash a usage hint rather than silently doing nothing.

Extend the `@type result` union with the four new tuples.

### Bot Name Resolution

The owner supplies the **bare** name as it was given at mint time (e.g. `/subscribe-bot claude-code-max`). The command prepends the `mcp-` prefix internally to match the bot username (`mcp-claude-code-max`), then resolves to a user where `username == "mcp-#{name}"` **and** `is_bot: true`.

- A non-matching name is a user error, not a silent no-op: flash `"No bot named 'claude-code-max' found"`.
- Requiring `is_bot: true` prevents the command from being used to subscribe arbitrary human users (that is a separate concern with its own invite/join flows).

### Handle Stage (`ChatLive.Index`)

Two new clauses in the `"send_message"` handler, structured exactly like `/decide`:

```elixir
{:subscribe_bot, bot_name} ->
  if FunWithFlags.enabled?(:bot_subscription, for: user) do
    # 1. authorize: owner of the active channel (manage_members)
    # 2. resolve bot user by "mcp-#{bot_name}" + is_bot
    # 3. Chat.Members.add_bot_member(channel_id, actor_id, bot_user_id)
    # 4. flash success (with channel_id) or a specific error; clear input
  else
    # Flag off: behave like an unrecognised command — do not leak the feature.
    {:noreply, put_flash(socket, :error, "Unknown command: /subscribe-bot")}
  end

{:unsubscribe_bot, bot_name} ->
  # same shape, calls Chat.Members.remove_bot_member/3 (delegates to kick_member/3)
```

The flag-off branch reproduces the `/decide` discipline (`index.ex:366-368`): an off flag responds `"Unknown command"`, never `"permission denied"`, so the command's existence is not leaked when the feature is dark.

## Context Layer (`Chat.Members`)

Keep the LiveView thin; put authorization and the insert/delete in the context where the rest of member management lives.

### New: `add_bot_member/3`

```elixir
def add_bot_member(channel_id, actor_id, bot_user_id) do
  with :ok <- authorize(actor_id, channel_id, :manage_members),
       {:ok, channel} <- public_channel(channel_id),
       :ok <- ensure_bot(bot_user_id) do
    %Subscription{}
    |> Subscription.changeset(%{user_id: bot_user_id, channel_id: channel.id, role: "member"})
    |> Repo.insert(on_conflict: :nothing, conflict_target: [:user_id, :channel_id])
    |> case do
      {:ok, %Subscription{user_id: nil}} -> {:ok, :already_subscribed}
      {:ok, subscription} -> {:ok, subscription}
      {:error, changeset} -> {:error, changeset}
    end
  end
end
```

- Reuses the existing private `authorize/3` (the same `manage_members` gate as `update_member_role` and `kick_member`).
- `public_channel/1` rejects private channels (`{:error, :private_channel_not_supported}`), mirroring the webhook constraint.
- `ensure_bot/1` rejects a non-bot target (`{:error, :not_a_bot}`).
- The nil-id ghost-struct guard is honored — a conflict returns `{:ok, :already_subscribed}` rather than treating the nil-id struct as a real row (per CLAUDE.md "Ecto upsert safety").
- Role is always `"member"`. **Pitfall:** the MCP server's `check_membership` treats any role as present, but the actual send re-checks `can?(role, :send_message)` which needs member+. A bot added as `viewer` would pass the membership gate and silently fail to send — so this function hardcodes `"member"`.

### New: `remove_bot_member/3`

Delegates to the existing `kick_member/3` (already `manage_members`-gated, already guards `cannot_kick_owner`), or a thin wrapper that additionally asserts `is_bot` for symmetry. No new authorization logic.

## Return Value: Channel Coordinates, Never Config

`/subscribe-bot` does **not** return MCP harness config. The harness config (server URL + bearer) is a global property of the bot, set once at `hermes mcp add`; subscribing the bot to additional channels does not change it. The bearer also cannot be recovered — only its hash is stored — and anything a slash command emits as a posted message would be Snowflake-ordered, broadcast, batch-persisted, and FTS/embedding-indexed, which is an unacceptable home for a credential.

What the agent genuinely gains from a subscribe is the **channel id** it may now target. The command surfaces that as a private flash to the owner (ephemeral, owner-only, never a posted message):

```
✓ claude-code-max subscribed to #engineering
  channel_id: 7283910011223344  (use as the target for send_message / reply_to_thread)
```

Connection config remains the responsibility of token-mint time (where the raw token is in hand), out of scope for this spec.

## Feedback Channel: Flash, Not System Message

Feedback is a **private flash to the owner**, not a system message posted into the channel. Rationale:

- The webhook subscription path posts nothing — flash-only is the lower-surface, consistent default.
- The typical setup is a single operator; a broadcast "bot was added" message has no audience.
- Keeping the `channel_id` hint in an ephemeral flash avoids persisting coordinates into channel history.

A visible system message ("`claude-code-max` was added by `@david`") is deliberately **not** included; it would only matter in channels with other humans who should notice the bot's arrival, which is not the target scenario. This can be revisited if multi-human channels become a use case.

## Feature Flag & Rollout

Gated behind `:bot_subscription` FunWithFlags flag:

- **Flag off** — Both commands behave as unrecognised commands (`"Unknown command: /subscribe-bot"`); the feature's existence is not leaked. `Chat.Members.add_bot_member/3` is unreferenced from any live surface.
- **Flag on** — Owners (admin+ via `manage_members`) can subscribe/unsubscribe MCP bots in public channels they manage.
- **Per-user gating** — Enable for the operator account first for validation.
- **Rollback:** Disabling the flag re-darkens both commands. Subscriptions already created remain valid (a bot stays a member); to fully reverse, the owner runs `/unsubscribe-bot <name>` per channel while the flag is on, or the rows are deleted directly. No migration is involved, so rollback is flag-only.

Per CLAUDE.md, all user-facing surfaces are gated from the start: parser clauses still parse (harmless), but both the LiveView handler and the absence of any other caller of `add_bot_member/3` keep the behavior fully behind the flag.

## Authorization Summary

| Concern | Decision | Rationale |
|---------|----------|-----------|
| Who may subscribe a bot | `manage_members` (admin+, level 3) | Reuses the existing gate for all member mutations; consistent with `update_member_role`/`kick_member`. Owner (level 4) is included. |
| Channel visibility | Public channels only | Mirrors the webhook constraint; private-channel bot subscription is out of scope. |
| Target user | Must be `is_bot: true` | Prevents the command from subscribing arbitrary humans; bots have their own membership path. |
| Subscription role | Always `"member"` | `viewer` would pass `check_membership` but fail `can?(:send_message)` — silent write failure. |
| Identity binding | Resolve `mcp-<name>` to the bot user the harness token points at | Subscribing the wrong bot user → harness connects as bot A, authorized as bot B → `"Not a member"`. |

## Testing Strategy

### Integration Test (full path — required)

Per CLAUDE.md's spec-driven acceptance rule, exercise the full producer → consumer path, not the parser in isolation:

Owner runs `/subscribe-bot <name>` in a public channel → assert a `subscriptions` row exists for the bot user in that channel → authenticate an MCP request as that bot's token → call `send_message` → assert it succeeds (no `"Not a member"`). This proves the command actually unlocks the MCP write path, not just that a row was inserted.

### Unit Tests

- `SlashCommand` — parses `/subscribe-bot name`, `/subscribe-bot` (help), `/unsubscribe-bot name`, `/unsubscribe-bot` (help), and unknown variants.
- `SlashCommand` — bot name with surrounding whitespace is trimmed.
- `Chat.Members.add_bot_member/3` — happy path inserts `role: "member"`.
- `Chat.Members.add_bot_member/3` — non-owner/non-admin actor → `{:error, :unauthorized}`.
- `Chat.Members.add_bot_member/3` — private channel → `{:error, :private_channel_not_supported}`.
- `Chat.Members.add_bot_member/3` — non-bot target → `{:error, :not_a_bot}`.
- `Chat.Members.add_bot_member/3` — already subscribed → `{:ok, :already_subscribed}` (nil-id ghost-struct guard), idempotent.
- `Chat.Members.remove_bot_member/3` — removes the row; cannot remove a non-member.
- `ChatLive.Index` — flag off → `/subscribe-bot` flashes `"Unknown command"`, inserts nothing (no feature leak).
- `ChatLive.Index` — flag on, non-matching name → flashes `"No bot named '…' found"`.
- `ChatLive.Index` — flag on, success → flash includes the channel id; input cleared.

## Module Summary

| Layer | Module | Change |
|-------|--------|--------|
| Parser | `ChatLive.SlashCommand` | Modify (add `subscribe-bot` / `unsubscribe-bot` clauses + `@type` union entries) |
| LiveView | `ChatLive.Index` (`"send_message"` handler) | Modify (add `{:subscribe_bot, _}` / `{:unsubscribe_bot, _}` clauses, flag-gated like `/decide`) |
| Context | `Chat.Members` | Modify (add `add_bot_member/3`; add `remove_bot_member/3` delegating to `kick_member/3`; private `public_channel/1`, `ensure_bot/1`) |
| Accounts | `Accounts` | No change (reuse `search_users/2` / `get_user` for name resolution) |
| Schema | `Chat.Subscription` | No change (existing `role`/`on_conflict` semantics reused) |
| Gate | `:bot_subscription` flag | New |
| Migration | — | None (no schema change) |
acceptance_criteria: Parser handles /subscribe-bot, /unsubscribe-bot (+help/unknown variants). Chat.Members.add_bot_member/3 inserts role=member, gated on manage_members, public-channel-only, is_bot-only, idempotent (ghost-struct guard). LiveView handler flag-gated like /decide (flag off => 'Unknown command', no leak). Success flashes channel_id, never a token/config, never a posted message. Integration test: owner runs command => subscriptions row exists => MCP send_message as that bot succeeds.
notes: Reuses Integrations.Webhooks.subscribe_bot/2 pattern and existing manage_members gate. Role hardcoded 'member' to avoid viewer silent-write trap. Status: design complete, ready to implement.
close_reason: Implemented per spec: parser, BotSubscription handler, Chat.Members.add/remove_bot_member, Accounts.get_bot_by_username, flag-gated :bot_subscription, full producer->consumer MCP integration test. Spec deviation: nil-id ghost guard inapplicable (Subscription has composite PK) — existence pre-check instead.

title: Sous, Factory, Analytics, Markdown contexts missing Boundary enforcement
description: Four context root modules do NOT 'use Boundary' and are NOT in the mix.exs boundary :ignore list, so they sit entirely outside compile-time architectural enforcement: Slackex.Sous (lib/slackex/sous.ex), Slackex.Factory (lib/slackex/factory.ex), Slackex.Analytics (lib/slackex/analytics.ex), Slackex.Markdown (lib/slackex/markdown.ex). 14 other contexts use Boundary. mix boundary.spec lists 10 boundaries with no Sous/Factory/Analytics/Markdown. Evidence it is an OVERSIGHT not intent: (1) not in the curated :ignore list (which documents intentional exclusions with comments); (2) consistent project convention to bound every context; (3) all four were created at/after markdown 2026-03-06 and the last boundary-add was integrations 2026-03-19 — factory 03-29, analytics 04-01, sous 05-27 all post-date it and were never retrofitted; (4) no comment/ADR documents a deliberate exclusion. Impact: these contexts' deps are unconstrained (can reach into other contexts' internals uncaught) and have no declared exports, so cross-context calls into them are unchecked. The gap is SILENT — Boundary is not configured to warn on unclassified modules, so compile stays clean.
design: Add 'use Boundary, deps: [...], exports: [...]' to each of the four root modules with the minimal correct dep/export set (derive deps from mix boundary.find_external_deps and actual call sites; markdown may instead belong in :ignore as a leaf utility — decide per-module). Then add a guard so NEW contexts cannot silently skip it: either enable a Boundary setting that flags unclassified modules, or extend the /new-feature scaffold to emit 'use Boundary'. Note markdown decision: bound it as a context vs treat as leaf (like Vault) and add to :ignore with a comment.
acceptance_criteria: All four root modules are either bounded (use Boundary with correct deps/exports) or explicitly in mix.exs :ignore with a justifying comment; mix boundary.spec reflects the decision; a mechanism exists so a newly-created context cannot silently lack Boundary; mix compile --warnings-as-errors stays clean.
notes:
PREVENTION GUARD DONE (commit f8156bd): /new-feature scaffold now has a mandatory Step 2 requiring any new context root module to 'use Boundary' (deps/exports) or be added to mix.exs :ignore with a comment, plus a verify-checklist item and an Important note. This closes the silent-recurrence half of this issue.

REMAINING SCOPE (this issue stays open for it): retrofit the 4 EXISTING unbounded contexts — Slackex.Sous, Slackex.Factory, Slackex.Analytics, Slackex.Markdown. For each: add 'use Boundary' with minimal deps (from mix boundary.find_external_deps + call sites) and a narrow exports list; OR, for a true leaf utility (Markdown is the candidate, cf. Slackex.Vault), add it to mix.exs boundary :ignore with a justifying comment. Verify each appears in mix boundary.spec and mix compile --warnings-as-errors stays clean. Expect Boundary violations to surface once bounded (e.g. unexpected cross-context reach) — fix or whitelist deliberately.
close_reason: All 4 contexts bounded + Ops.SystemSummary (5th, found during work). Root-cause discovery: boundary compiler was inert since 914f1f7 (wrong compiler order) — fixed, all 257 surfaced violations resolved or deliberately whitelisted, liveness contract test added. Follow-ups: slackex-dfa (Accounts->Notifications leak), slackex-oyo (real SlackexWeb deps).

title: Bump GitHub Actions off deprecated Node 20 runtime before forced Node 24 cutover
description: GitHub is forcing all JavaScript actions to Node 24 on 2026-06-16; Node 20 is removed from runners on 2026-09-16. CI annotations on the v0.9.25 deploy run (26983946203) flagged these Node-20 actions in .github/workflows/ci-deploy.yml: actions/checkout@v4, actions/cache@v4, docker/build-push-action@v6, docker/login-action@v3, docker/setup-buildx-action@v3. Pin/upgrade each to a release that ships a Node 24 entrypoint (or set FORCE_JAVASCRIPT_ACTIONS_TO_NODE24=true as a stopgap). Verify a tag deploy still goes green after the bump. Ref: https://github.blog/changelog/2025-09-19-deprecation-of-node-20-on-github-actions-runners/
acceptance_criteria: All JS-based actions in .github/workflows/ run on Node 24; no Node-20 deprecation annotations on a fresh CI run; a tag deploy completes successfully.
close_reason: Bumped all flagged actions to Node 24 majors (checkout@v5, cache@v5, setup-buildx@v4, login@v4, build-push@v7). Master Quality run 27023221834 and v0.9.26 deploy run 27023635535 both green with zero Node-20 deprecation annotations; full tag deploy validated end-to-end.

title: PWA chrome: purple -> Loom black/gold
description: PWA chrome (manifest theme/background colors, theme-color meta tags, offline page, app icons) still uses the pre-Loom purple (#5D4D8F / #2A2640 / #E8A835). Update to the warm-charcoal+gold Loom palette: surfaces #131109 (header) and #0b0a07 (body splash), light surface #fbf7eb, gold accent #e8c547 on the icon mark only. CRITICAL: bump service-worker CACHE_NAME (v3->v4) so existing installs reinstall and pick up the new offline page. Regenerate the 3 purple PNG app icons to charcoal bg + gold serif T.
close_reason: PWA chrome restyled to Loom black/gold; icons+favicon regenerated; SW cache bumped

title: Decompose ChatLive.Index god-LiveView by concern (on_mount + attach_hook)
description: lib/slackex_web/live/chat_live/index.ex is a god-LiveView: 48 handle_event + 39 handle_info + 8 handle_params = 95 callback clauses, ~45 assigns in mount, spanning ~14 distinct feature concerns (messages, threads, reactions, search, summary streaming, DM-safety/reports, profiles, push, presence/typing, channels, quick-switcher, appearance, sous cards, catchup). Clause-level code is good (delegates to Chat/Accounts/Helpers) — the problem is concern concentration in one module. The 'extracted to keep cyclomatic complexity within credo budget' comment on dispatch_message is the tell: complexity pressure escaped sideways into more functions in the same file because module-level decomposition is overdue. Tier 1 (extract push-notification DeviceToken persistence + health derivation into Slackex.Notifications.DeviceTokens / PushHealth) is DONE — this issue tracks Tier 2/3.
design: Tier 2 (idiomatic, behavior-preserving): split callbacks by concern using on_mount + Phoenix.LiveView.attach_hook(:handle_event|:handle_info). Each concern becomes a module with on_mount/4 that assigns its defaults and attaches hooks returning {:halt, socket} when it handles the event/msg, {:cont, socket} to pass through. Proposed grouping (14->7): Messages (stays in LiveView: send/edit/delete/pin/reaction/load_more + message.new/edited + link previews), PushNotifications (9 push events + infos), Profiles, DmSafety (accept/decline/block + report modal), SearchPanel, Summary (streaming infos), PresenceTyping (typing/heartbeats/presence). Quick-switcher/appearance/sidebar/sous fold into nearest or a small Chrome hook. Do Summary or PushNotifications first as the pattern-setter, then the rest is mechanical. Tier 3: promote remaining inline modals (report, edit-profile, appearance) to self-contained LiveComponents owning their own assigns+events (search/thread/sidebar already are), which removes those assigns from mount.
acceptance_criteria: index.ex reduced to mount skeleton + message pipeline + render; each extracted concern is its own module with its assigns/events/infos; full chat_live suite stays green (behavior-preserving); mount assign count materially reduced.

title: Homelab observability gaps found during /health spike RCA
description: Follow-ups from 2026-06-12 stall investigation: (1) UNIDENTIFIED 30-MIN RESTART LOOP: a slackex stack container (tempo or grafana by elimination) restarted 598 times Jun 5→Jun 12, clean exit 0, median 1802s interval — destroyed by the v0.9.27 deploy before it could be named. Currently 0 restarts post-deploy (23 min in); if it resumes, identify via 'docker events --filter event=die' and fix. (2) KUMA CANNOT SEPARATE WAN FROM APP: the SlackEx/Hexis Golf monitors are the only DNS-name + Cloudflare-path monitors, so WAN/CF degradation is indistinguishable from app downtime. Add a LAN-direct check of caddy (and/or app /health via LAN IP) so external-path noise is attributable. (3) caddy access logs disabled — no request-level durations at the proxy; consider enabling for chat.davewil.dev. (4) pocket-pro-worker (different project) healthcheck uses pgrep which doesn't exist in its image — failing exec every 10s since Jun 4, 354 journal lines/hr, 823MB journal. Fix its healthcheck or remove it. (5) journald hygiene: vacuum old logs.

title: Split Moderation Act into pure Plan + IO Run (Gather→Decide→Plan→Run)
description: perform_report/4 in lib/slackex/chat/moderation.ex welds two beats together: pure Ecto.Multi assembly and the Repo.transaction that runs it. Extract the assembly into a pure plan_report/4 returning %Ecto.Multi{}, leaving run_report/1 as the entire IO shell. Refines the documented Gather→Decide→Act pattern to a 4-beat Gather→Decide→Plan→Run.
design: plan_report/4 = top half of perform_report/4 moved verbatim (maybe_* helpers come along unchanged), returns %Ecto.Multi{}, no Repo call. run_report/1 = the Repo.transaction + result-normalising case. create_abuse_report/3 becomes a 4-beat pipe: gather_context (IO) -> Rules.evaluate (pure) -> plan_report (pure) -> run_report (IO). Two caveats to record in code/docs: (1) does NOT retire the projection compromise — that's a Gather-timing issue and survives any behaviour-preserving refactor; Plan/Run is orthogonal. (2) plan_report isn't strictly pure: report_changeset calls Snowflake.generate(); for strict purity inject the id from the shell. The :dm_restriction/:admin_flag steps still wrap Multi.run IO closures — plan proves the steps exist, not their bodies; full effect-as-data (declarative tuples + interpreter) is overkill for 4 effects, the Multi already is the effect-value. Also update CLAUDE.md Gather→Decide→Act section with the Plan/Run refinement.
acceptance_criteria: abuse_report_flow_test.exs unchanged and green (behaviour preserved); new pure unit test asserts plan_report's Ecto.Multi.to_list step names for a given ModerationAction with no DB; CLAUDE.md pattern doc updated to mention the Plan/Run split; credo --strict + contract gate pass.

title: Deep dive: ChannelServer terminate-flush ownership crash during teardown sweep (suite-context only)
description: 2-3 'ChannelServer flush crashed: cannot find ownership ... mode :manual' log lines per full-suite run. Evidence collected 2026-06-10: (1) crash fires DURING shutdown_channel_servers' terminate-flush — probe showed crashing pid == swept pid; (2) sandbox owner process is ALIVE at teardown entry (probe, 27/27 teardowns) yet the flush sees mode :manual; (3) does NOT reproduce in isolated file runs (3x subscribe_bot_test clean) — full-suite/cross-file context required; (4) impact is nil: flushed rows belong to the dying test's rolled-back transaction, channel ids unique per test, suite green (1649/0). Open question: why does the ownership lookup resolve to :manual mid-sweep when the shared owner is alive — suspect cross-file window interaction (stop_owner of a prior file racing Horde supervisor calls, or ReadRepo vs Repo mode mismatch). Sweep already hardened (registry+supervisor union, bounded re-sweep, 038e65c follow-up). Characterization documented in data_case.ex KNOWN RESIDUAL comment.

title: test_helper.exs global flag-enable block is a sandbox-rolled-back no-op
description: test/test_helper.exs enables :message_search, :channel_summarization, :sous inside Sandbox.checkout/checkin — the checkout wraps writes in a sandbox transaction that rolls back at checkin, so the 'enable all feature flags in tests' block has never done anything. De-facto behavior: flags are OFF unless a file's setup enables them, and many setups do exactly that. Decide: (a) make it real via checkout(repo, sandbox: false) — then audit per-file enables and any tests assuming flags off; or (b) delete the block and keep per-file enables as the explicit convention. Option (b) matches current reality with zero behavioral risk. Discovered during the on_exit teardown-safety sweep (slackex-luc follow-up).

title: Declare real deps for SlackexWeb boundary (currently unchecked)
description: SlackexWeb uses check: [in: false, out: false] (the valid replacement for the never-valid 'ignore: true'). This means context exports lists are not exercised by their main caller. Enhancement: declare deps: [all contexts] on SlackexWeb and fix the export-list violations that surface (255 references were observed when web was checked during slackex-n3c). Makes exports: lists real contracts.

title: Move default notification-preference creation out of Accounts (boundary leak)
description: Slackex.Accounts.register_user calls Slackex.Notifications.Preference.create_default_for_user (accounts.ex:28). Adding Slackex.Notifications to Accounts deps would create a cycle (Notifications -> Chat -> Accounts), so the reference is whitelisted via dirty_xrefs in the Accounts boundary. Proper fix: move preference bootstrap out of Accounts — e.g. the registration caller orchestrates both, or Notifications reacts to a user-created event. Remove the dirty_xrefs entry when done.

title: Architecture docs: fill two deferred completeness gaps
description: The comprehensive architecture doc set (docs/architecture/, 29 docs) deferred two minor coverage gaps flagged by the completeness critic. Both are partially covered elsewhere; standalone docs would aid discovery. (1) Infrastructure utilities: lib/slackex/infrastructure/rate_limiter.ex has no dedicated coverage (snowflake.ex is fully covered by deep-dive-snowflake-partitioning.md). (2) A dedicated lib/slackex/pipeline/ doc for BatchWriter lifecycle/writer-epoch fencing — currently split across message-pipeline-and-persistence.md, deep-dive-multi-node-horde.md, and deep-dive-pipeline-events-bridge.md. Decision to defer: content is not missing, only un-consolidated; the workers gap (the third critic finding) WAS filled with background-jobs-and-workers.md.
acceptance_criteria: Either a rate-limiter section/doc exists, or README explicitly notes infrastructure utilities live in cross-cutting/deep-dive docs; pipeline/BatchWriter has a clear single home or an explicit cross-reference note.

title: Sous: let a viewer override the AI-generated Prism facet text
description: The Facet Drawer's per-role Prism text is AI-generated (Slice B2). It should be clearer in the UI that this text is AI-generated, and a viewer should be able to override/edit it with their own wording — the human has final say over how a decision is framed for their role. Currently set_facet_text/3 is the only write path and facet_text comes solely from FacetWorker (:facet_generated event). A human override is a distinct path.
design: Add a :facet_overridden event type + extend set_facet_text (or a sibling command) so a viewer-authored facet_text wins over the AI one and is NOT re-staled/regenerated by prompt_version bumps or :state_changed (an override is sticky until the human clears it). UI: label the drawer text as AI-generated (e.g. a small 'AI' chip + 'edit' affordance); on edit, persist as override. Pill-state derivation (WorkItemFacet.state/1) needs an :overridden state so it renders distinctly from fresh/stale/generating. Fits the B-later bucket noted in slice-b2 design doc §2 (alongside role-management UI and per-user personalisation).
acceptance_criteria: A viewer can edit a Prism facet; the edited text persists and survives a prompt_version bump and state changes; the UI distinguishes AI-generated vs human-overridden; clearing an override returns the row to AI regeneration.

title: Review: 2026-06-12 external agent repo review findings
description: Structured review performed by Grok. Captures observations, confirms high maturity, cross-checks CLAUDE.md rules (esp. on_conflict ghosts, boundaries, god-LiveView, pure cores), validates existing beads coverage, and surfaces any additional low-severity hygiene notes not yet tracked. No code changes; suggestions primarily point at existing open beads (cm5, 8xx, oyo, dfa, byg) or minor polish (logged rescues, docs drift checks).
